tier_1_regimens.setting: Perioperative chemotherapy | First-line metastatic | Subsequent-line metastatic

drugs.name: Fluorouracil
drugs.dose: 400 mg/m2 IV bolus on Day 1, followed by 1200 mg/m2/day x 2 days (total 2400 mg/m2 over 46-48 hours)
drugs.schedule: Day 1; continuous infusion x 2 days; repeat every 2 weeks

tier_1_regimens.notes: Modified FOLFOX6 is the standard doublet backbone for perioperative and metastatic chemotherapy. Leucovorin 400 mg/m2 is equivalent to levoleucovorin 200 mg/m2. Oxaliplatin may be infused over a shorter time at a rate of 1 mg/m2/min. Discontinuation of oxaliplatin should be strongly considered after 3 to 4 months of therapy (or sooner for unacceptable neurotoxicity) while maintaining other agents until time of progression in the metastatic setting.

drugs.name: Fluorouracil
drugs.dose: 1200 mg/m2/day x 2 days (total 2400 mg/m2 over 46-48 hours)
drugs.schedule: Day 1; continuous infusion x 2 days; repeat every 2 weeks

tier_1_regimens.notes: Modified FOLFOX7 omits the 5-FU bolus compared to Modified FOLFOX6, using only continuous infusion fluorouracil.

drugs.name: Fluorouracil
drugs.dose: 400 mg/m2 IV bolus on Day 1, followed by 1200 mg/m2/day x 2 days (total 2400 mg/m2 over 46-48 hours)
drugs.schedule: Day 1; continuous infusion x 2 days; repeat every 2 weeks

tier_1_regimens.notes: Bevacizumab may be safely given at a rate of 0.5 mg/kg/min (5 mg/kg over 10 minutes). Bevacizumab is the preferred anti-angiogenic agent and can be continued into the second line of therapy and beyond. There should be at least a 6-week interval between the last dose of bevacizumab and elective surgery, and re-initiation of bevacizumab should be delayed at least 6 to 8 weeks postoperatively.

drugs.name: Fluorouracil
drugs.dose: 400 mg/m2 IV bolus on Day 1, followed by 1200 mg/m2/day x 2 days (total 2400 mg/m2 over 46-48 hours)
drugs.schedule: Day 1; continuous infusion x 2 days; repeat every 2 weeks

tier_1_regimens.notes: EGFR inhibitors include cetuximab and panitumumab. An EGFR inhibitor should not be continued beyond progression of disease. There is no efficacy benefit in changing the EGFR inhibitor at the time of progression.

drugs.name: Fluorouracil
drugs.dose: 400 mg/m2 IV bolus on Day 1, followed by 1200 mg/m2/day x 2 days (total 2400 mg/m2 over 46-48 hours)
drugs.schedule: Day 1; continuous infusion x 2 days; repeat every 2 weeks

drugs.name: Cetuximab
drugs.dose: 400 mg/m2 IV over 2 hours first infusion, followed by 250 mg/m2 IV over 60 minutes weekly; OR 500 mg/m2 IV over 2 hours, Day 1, every 2 weeks (preferred for every 2 weeks)

tier_1_regimens.notes: Patients with any known KRAS mutation (exons 2, 3, and 4) or NRAS mutation (exons 2, 3, and 4) should not be treated with cetuximab or panitumumab. BRAF V600E mutation testing is required; BRAF V600E mutation makes response to cetuximab or panitumumab highly unlikely.

tier_1_regimens.setting: Perioperative chemotherapy | First-line metastatic | Subsequent-line metastatic
tier_1_regimens.notes: The majority of safety and efficacy data for this regimen have been developed in Europe, where a capecitabine starting dose of 1000 mg/m2 twice daily for 14 days, repeated every 21 days, is standard. Evidence suggests that North American patients may experience greater toxicity with capecitabine (as well as with other fluoropyrimidines) than European patients, and may require a lower dose of capecitabine. For information on DPYD testing and fluoropyrimidine-associated toxicity, see the NCCN Guidelines for Colon Cancer.
Bevacizumab may be safely given at a rate of 0.5 mg/kg/min (7.5 mg/kg over 15 minutes).

drugs.name: Cetuximab
drugs.dose: 400 mg/m2 IV over 2 hours first infusion, followed by 250 mg/m2 IV over 60 minutes weekly; OR 500 mg/m2 IV over 2 hours, Day 1, every 2 weeks (preferred for every 2 weeks)

tier_1_regimens.notes: The addition of FOLFOX or CAPEOX to encorafenib plus cetuximab or panitumumab can be considered for patients without residual neuropathy and good prior tolerance of chemotherapy.
Panitumumab is given every 2 weeks while FOLFOX/CAPEOX is given on a 3-week cycle; the regimens are combined.

tier_1_regimens.notes: FOLFIRI is an irinotecan-based backbone used as first-line therapy and in subsequent lines. Irinotecan should be used with caution in patients with Gilbert syndrome or elevated serum bilirubin.

tier_1_regimens.notes: Bevacizumab is the preferred anti-angiogenic agent. An anti-angiogenic agent other than bevacizumab (ziv-aflibercept or ramucirumab) may be substituted in subsequent lines.

There are no data to suggest activity of FOLFIRI-ziv-aflibercept or FOLFIRI-ramucirumab in a patient whose disease has progressed on FOLFIRI-bevacizumab, or vice versa.

drugs.name: Fluorouracil
drugs.dose: 400 mg/m2 IV push Day 1, followed by 1200 mg/m2/day x 2 days (total 2400 mg/m2 over 46 hours)
drugs.schedule: Day 1; continuous infusion x 2 days; repeat every 2 weeks

tier_1_regimens.guideline_category: Consider (neoadjuvant T4, N+ only) | Other recommended (first-line metastatic)
tier_1_regimens.notes: FOLFIRINOX is recommended instead of FOLFOXIRI because FOLFOXIRI uses a high dose of fluorouracil (3200 mg/m2 over 48 hours). Patients in the United States have been shown to have greater toxicity with fluorouracil. The dose of fluorouracil (2400 mg/m2 over 46 hours) is a starting dose consistent with the dose recommended in FOLFOX or FOLFIRI and should be strongly considered for U.S. patients. In the neoadjuvant setting, this regimen is recommended only for cT4, N+ disease due to higher toxicity compared to FOLFOX or CAPEOX.

drugs.name: Fluorouracil
drugs.dose: 1200 mg/m2/day x 2 days (total 2400 mg/m2 over 46 hours)
drugs.schedule: Day 1; continuous infusion x 2 days; repeat every 2 weeks

tier_1_regimens.notes: Modified FOLFIRINOX omits the 5-FU bolus and uses a lower irinotecan dose (150 mg/m2) compared to standard FOLFIRINOX.

drugs.name: Fluorouracil

tier_1_regimens.notes: FOLFOXIRI plus bevacizumab versus FOLFIRI plus bevacizumab as first-line treatment of patients with mCRC (TRIBE study).

drugs.schedule: 1 hour after start of leucovorin, Days 1, 8, 15, 22, 29, and 36; repeat every 8 weeks

tier_1_regimens.notes: Weekly bolus regimen. Leucovorin 500 mg/m2 is given over 2 hours, followed by 5-FU bolus 500 mg/m2 given 1 hour after start of leucovorin.

drugs.name: Fluorouracil

drugs.schedule: Day 1; continuous infusion x 2 days; repeat every 2 weeks

tier_1_regimens.notes: This is the infusional 5-FU/leucovorin backbone without oxaliplatin or irinotecan.

drugs.schedule: 1 hour after start of leucovorin; repeat weekly

tier_1_regimens.notes: Weekly regimen with low-dose leucovorin. Alternative: Fluorouracil 2600 mg/m2 by 24-hour infusion plus Leucovorin 500 mg/m2, repeat every week.

drugs.name: Leucovorin
drugs.schedule: Day 1; repeat every 2 weeks (or other schedule per backbone used)

Irinotecan monotherapy at various schedules. Single-agent fluoropyrimidine is not recommended after progression on a fluoropyrimidine-containing regimen. Irinotecan should be used with caution in patients with Gilbert syndrome or elevated serum bilirubin.

drugs.name: Irinotecan
drugs.schedule: Day 1; repeat every 2 weeks (or other schedule)

Panitumumab combined with irinotecan for patients with KRAS wild-type mCRC refractory to standard chemotherapy.

drugs.name: Cetuximab

Panitumumab monotherapy for patients who cannot tolerate irinotecan-based therapy.

drugs.name: Regorafenib

drugs.name: Regorafenib
drugs.dose: 80 mg PO daily Days 1-7, followed by 120 mg PO daily Days 8-14, followed by 160 mg PO daily Days 15-21 (first cycle only); Subsequent cycles: 160 mg PO daily Days 1-21

tier_1_regimens.notes: Dose-escalation strategy to improve tolerability of regorafenib.

drugs.schedule: Twice daily, Days 1-5 and 8-12; repeat every 28 days

drugs.name: Trifluridine and Tipiracil
drugs.dose: 35 mg/m2 up to a maximum dose of 80 mg per dose (based on the trifluridine component)
drugs.schedule: Twice daily, Days 1-5 and 8-12; repeat every 28 days

tier_1_regimens.guideline_category: Preferred (bevacizumab combo preferred over TAS-102 alone)

drugs.name: Trastuzumab
drugs.dose: 8 mg/kg IV loading dose on Day 1 of cycle 1, followed by 6 mg/kg IV every 21 days

drugs.name: Pertuzumab
drugs.dose: 840 mg IV loading dose on Day 1 of cycle 1, followed by 420 mg IV every 21 days

tier_1_regimens.notes: HER2 (ERBB2) overexpression/amplification must be documented. Anti-HER2 therapy with signal transduction inhibition (eg, pertuzumab/trastuzumab) is indicated for patients with HER2 overexpression/amplification, IHC 3+, IHC2+/ISH+, or HER2 overexpressed/amplified (detected by NGS) cancers that are also KRAS, NRAS, and BRAF wild-type.

drugs.name: Trastuzumab
drugs.dose: 8 mg/kg IV loading dose on Day 1 of cycle 1, followed by 6 mg/kg IV every 21 days

tier_1_regimens.notes: Tucatinib is an oral HER2-selective tyrosine kinase inhibitor combined with trastuzumab for HER2-amplified mCRC.

Fam-trastuzumab deruxtecan-nxki (T-DXd) is only indicated in HER2-overexpressed tumors (IHC 3+). Some activity was seen after previous treatment with a HER2-targeted regimen. May not be indicated in patients with underlying lung issues due to lung toxicity (drug-related deaths from interstitial lung disease were reported in 3.5% of patients on the DESTINY-CRC01 trial).

drugs.name: Cetuximab
drugs.dose: 400 mg/m2 IV followed by 250 mg/m2 IV weekly; OR 500 mg/m2 IV every 2 weeks

tier_1_regimens.notes: Patients with BRAF V600E-mutant cancers should receive an encorafenib-containing regimen during one line of therapy. A chemotherapy-containing option is preferred in the first-line metastatic setting.
Encorafenib + panitumumab is a preferred option for BRAF V600E-mutated mCRC.

drugs.name: Fluorouracil
drugs.dose: 400 mg/m2 IV bolus on Day 1, followed by 1200 mg/m2/day x 2 days (total 2400 mg/m2 over 46-48 hours)

tier_1_regimens.notes: FOLFOX/Encorafenib + Cetuximab is a preferred first-line regimen for BRAF V600E-mutated mCRC. BRAF V600E regimen may be given with FOLFOX as subsequent-line therapy if no previous treatment with oxaliplatin or BRAF-targeting regimen.

drugs.name: Fluorouracil
drugs.dose: 400 mg/m2 IV bolus on Day 1, followed by 1200 mg/m2/day x 2 days (total 2400 mg/m2 over 46-48 hours)

tier_1_regimens.notes: Preferred first-line regimen for BRAF V600E-mutated mCRC. The addition of FOLFOX or CAPEOX can be considered for patients without residual neuropathy and good prior tolerance of chemotherapy.

drugs.name: Fluorouracil
drugs.dose: 400 mg/m2 IV bolus, followed by 1200 mg/m2/day x 2 days (total 2400 mg/m2 over 46-48 hours)

drugs.name: Fluorouracil

Adagrasib monotherapy for KRAS G12C-mutated mCRC. Some patients may have activity after previous treatment with a HER2-targeted regimen.
Sotorasib monotherapy for previously treated KRAS G12C-mutated CRC.

Alternative combination for KRAS G12C-mutated mCRC.

Sotorasib combined with panitumumab for KRAS G12C-mutated mCRC.

Entrectinib for patients with advanced or metastatic NTRK fusion-positive solid tumours.

drugs.name: Repotrectinib
drugs.dose: 160 mg PO daily for first 14 days, then increase to 160 mg PO twice daily

tier_1_regimens.notes: Repotrectinib showed activity in both NTRK TKI-naive and NTRK TKI-pretreated patients on the TRIDENT-1 trial.

drugs.name: Selpercatinib
drugs.dose: 160 mg PO twice daily (patients >=50 kg); OR 120 mg PO twice daily (patients <50 kg)

tier_1_regimens.notes: Selpercatinib is FDA-approved for patients with solid tumors harboring activating RET gene fusions. Caution should be used in interpreting RET gene fusion results from ctDNA/RNA assays following targeted therapy with EGFR inhibitors.

drugs.name: Fruquintinib
drugs.schedule: Daily on Days 1-21; repeat every 28 days

tier_1_regimens.notes: Fruquintinib is a selective inhibitor of vascular endothelial growth factor receptors (VEGFR) 1, 2, and 3 for refractory mCRC.

tier_1_regimens.setting: Any line of therapy (dMMR/MSI-H or POLE/POLD1 mutation with ultra-hypermutated phenotype)

drugs.name: Pembrolizumab
drugs.dose: 2 mg/kg IV every 3 weeks; OR 200 mg IV every 3 weeks; OR 400 mg IV every 6 weeks; OR 4 mg/kg IV every 6 weeks
drugs.schedule: Every 3 weeks or every 6 weeks (various schedules)

biomarkers.requirement: Mutation with ultra-hypermutated phenotype (eg, TMB >50 mut/Mb)

tier_1_regimens.notes: Patients should be followed closely for 10 weeks to assess for response. If disease response, consider discontinuing checkpoint inhibitor after 2 years of treatment. Some of these agents have not been specifically studied in CRC but are included in this Guideline based on the presumed class effect from data demonstrated in other cancer types. Pembrolizumab and berahyaluronidase alfa-pmph subcutaneous injection may be substituted for IV pembrolizumab.

tier_1_regimens.setting: Any line of therapy (dMMR/MSI-H or POLE/POLD1 mutation with ultra-hypermutated phenotype)

drugs.name: Nivolumab
drugs.dose: 3 mg/kg every 2 weeks; OR 240 mg IV every 2 weeks; OR 480 mg IV every 4 weeks; OR 6 mg/kg IV every 4 weeks
drugs.schedule: Every 2 weeks or every 4 weeks (various schedules)

tier_1_regimens.notes: Nivolumab and hyaluronidase-nvhy is not approved for concurrent use with IV ipilimumab; however, for nivolumab monotherapy, nivolumab and hyaluronidase-nvhy subcutaneous injection may be substituted for IV nivolumab.

tier_1_regimens.setting: Any line of therapy (dMMR/MSI-H or POLE/POLD1 mutation with ultra-hypermutated phenotype)

drugs.name: Nivolumab
drugs.schedule: Once every 3 weeks for four doses (with ipilimumab), followed by Nivolumab 3 mg/kg or 240 mg IV every 2 weeks or 480 mg IV every 4 weeks

drugs.name: Ipilimumab
drugs.schedule: Once every 3 weeks for four doses, then discontinue

tier_1_regimens.notes: Combination PD-1/CTLA-4 blockade has been associated with improved survival in unresectable or metastatic CRC, but also higher toxicity. Nivolumab plus ipilimumab is recommended after checkpoint inhibitor monotherapy was previously received.

drugs.name: Dostarlimab-gxly
drugs.dose: 500 mg IV every 3 weeks for 4 doses, followed by 1000 mg IV every 6 weeks
drugs.schedule: Every 3 weeks x 4, then every 6 weeks

tier_1_regimens.notes: Dostarlimab-gxly is an anti-PD-1 monoclonal antibody. In a phase II trial, all 12 patients with dMMR stage II or III rectal adenocarcinoma showed a clinical complete response to dostarlimab-gxly, with no patients requiring chemoradiotherapy or surgery. A larger follow-up of 49 patients in the rectal cancer cohort showed cCR in all, with 2-year RFS of 96%.
PD-1 inhibitor for dMMR/MSI-H tumors.

Neoadjuvant PD-1 blockade with toripalimab in dMMR/MSI-H locally advanced colorectal cancer.
Added as a category 2A recommendation in Version 2.2026. Some of these agents have not been specifically studied in CRC but are included based on presumed class effect.
PD-1 inhibitor for MSI-H/dMMR endometrial cancer and other solid tumors.

drugs.name: Atezolizumab
drugs.dose: 840 mg IV Day 1 every 14 days; OR 1200 mg IV Day 1 every 21 days; OR 1680 mg IV Day 1 every 28 days

tier_1_regimens.notes: PD-L1 inhibitor added as a category 2A recommendation in Version 2.2026. drugs.name: Fluorouracil
drugs.schedule: Daily on Days 1-5 or Days 1-7 for 5 weeks, with concurrent RT (45-54 Gy in 25-30 fractions)

tier_1_regimens.notes: Continuous infusion 5-FU at 225 mg/m2/day for 5 weeks with RT. This is a standard concurrent chemoradiation regimen for locally advanced rectal cancer. RT dosing: 45-54 Gy in 25-30 fractions. Small bowel max point dose should be limited to Dmax 55 Gy, V45 Gy should be <=150 cc for individual small bowel loops.

drugs.name: Capecitabine
drugs.schedule: Monday-Friday, on days of radiation treatment only, throughout the duration of RT (typically 28-30 treatment days); concurrent with RT (45-54 Gy in 25-30 fractions)

tier_1_regimens.notes: Capecitabine is equivalent to 5-FU in perioperative chemoradiation therapy. Capecitabine given concurrently with RT is listed as an acceptable alternative to infusional 5-FU in those patients who are able to manage the responsibilities inherent in self-administered, oral chemotherapy. Evidence suggests that North American patients may require a lower dose of capecitabine.

drugs.name: Radiation therapy
drugs.schedule: 5 fractions (5 Gy per fraction), typically over 1 week

trial_data.pfs: 3-year disease-related treatment failure: 23.7% TNT vs 30.4% standard (HR, 0.75; 95% CI, 0.60-0.95; P=.019)

tier_1_regimens.notes: Short-course RT (25 Gy in 5 fractions) can be considered for patients for preoperative radiation. For high-risk rectal cancer (cT4a or cT4b, EMVI, cN2, involved MRF, or enlarged lateral lymph nodes considered metastatic), the 5-year follow-up of the RAPIDO trial now indicates a statistically higher locoregional recurrence rate with short-course RT. Small bowel max point dose should be limited to D0.03 cc 26.5 Gy, and V20 Gy should be 50 cc for individual small bowel loops. A multidisciplinary evaluation, including a discussion of the need for downstaging and the possibility of long-term toxicity, is recommended when considering short-course RT.

drugs.name: Nivolumab
drugs.schedule: Every 2 weeks; OR 480 mg IV every 4 weeks; for up to 6 months total

tier_1_regimens.notes: For stage II-III dMMR/MSI-H or POLE/POLD1 rectal cancer, neoadjuvant checkpoint inhibitor immunotherapy is the preferred treatment. Disease status should be re-evaluated every 2-3 months. If complete clinical response is achieved, surveillance may be recommended. If persistent disease at 6 months, consider long-course chemoRT or short-course RT. If consistent response, continue checkpoint inhibitor immunotherapy for up to 6 months total.

drugs.name: Dostarlimab-gxly
drugs.schedule: Every 3 weeks for 4 doses, then 1000 mg every 6 weeks; for up to 6 months total

tier_1_regimens.notes: In a phase II trial of dostarlimab-gxly for 6 months in dMMR locally advanced rectal cancer, all 49 patients who completed treatment showed a cCR and elected to forgo surgery. Two-year RFS was 96%. This is a preferred option for neoadjuvant immunotherapy.

drugs.name: Pembrolizumab
drugs.schedule: Every 3 weeks; for up to 6 months total

tier_1_regimens.notes: Pembrolizumab for neoadjuvant treatment of localized dMMR/MSI-H solid tumors. Of 27 colorectal cancer patients, 8 had rectal adenocarcinoma. ORR was 82%.

drugs.name: Trifluridine and Tipiracil
drugs.dose: 35 mg/m2 up to a maximum dose of 80 mg per dose (based on the trifluridine component)
drugs.schedule: Twice daily, Days 1-5 and 8-12; repeat every 28 days

tier_1_regimens.notes: For disease that has progressed through all available regimens, trifluridine/tipiracil +/- bevacizumab is recommended, along with fruquintinib and regorafenib.

drugs.name: Fruquintinib
drugs.schedule: Daily on Days 1-21; repeat every 28 days

tier_1_regimens.notes: Selective VEGFR inhibitor for refractory mCRC.

drugs.name: Regorafenib
drugs.schedule: Daily on Days 1-21; repeat every 28 days

tier_2_handbook.definition:
Rectal cancer is a malignant epithelial neoplasm, most commonly adenocarcinoma, arising from the mucosal lining of the rectum. The rectum is anatomically defined as the portion of the large bowel lying below the peritoneal reflection, extending from the sacral promontory to the upper edge of the symphysis pubis as determined by magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) [REC-2 footnote k]. The rectum terminates at the superior border of the functional anal canal, defined as the palpable upper border of the anal sphincter and puborectalis muscles of the anorectal ring [MS-8]. The rectum is approximately 15 cm (6 inches) in length [MS-2].

Rectal cancer is staged using the American Joint Committee on Cancer (AJCC) Cancer Staging Manual, 8th Edition [ST-1, ST-2]. The TNM classification defines: T1 tumors invade the submucosa (through muscularis mucosae but not into muscularis propria); T2 tumors invade the muscularis propria; T3 tumors penetrate through the muscularis propria; T4a tumors directly penetrate to the surface of the visceral peritoneum; and T4b tumors directly invade or are adherent to other organs or structures. Regional lymph node classification includes N1a (1 positive node), N1b (2–3 positive nodes), N1c (tumor deposits without nodal metastasis), N2a (4–6 positive nodes), and N2b (≥7 positive nodes). Distant metastasis is classified as M0 (none) or M1 (present), with M1a (single distant site), M1b (multiple distant sites), and M1c (peritoneal surface metastasis) [ST-1].

The most common histologic type of rectal cancer is adenocarcinoma. Mucinous histology is also recognized and may have implications for imaging assessment [REC-A 3 of 5]. Molecular profiling has identified distinct biologic subtypes that dictate treatment approaches: proficient mismatch repair/microsatellite stable (pMMR/MSS) tumors, which represent the majority; deficient mismatch repair/microsatellite instability-high (dMMR/MSI-H) tumors; and tumors harboring polymerase proofreading domain mutations (POLE/POLD1) with ultra-hypermutated phenotype (tumor mutational burden [TMB] >50 mutations/megabase) [REC-2A, REC-B 7].

Rectal cancer differs from colon cancer in several critical respects: the rectum lacks a serosal covering, is in close proximity to pelvic structures and organs, presents technical challenges in achieving adequate surgical margins, and carries a higher risk of locoregional recurrence. These characteristics necessitate a distinct management approach that frequently incorporates neoadjuvant radiation therapy and total mesorectal excision (TME) [REC-C 1, REC-E]. The NCCN Guidelines for Rectal Cancer provide separate, disease-specific recommendations reflecting these unique considerations. T stage has more prognostic value than N stage in rectal cancer: patients with stage IIIA disease (T1–2) have longer rectal cancer-specific survival than patients with stage IIA (T3), IIB (T4a), or IIC (T4b) disease [MS-3].

subtypes.name: Proficient Mismatch Repair/Microsatellite Stable (pMMR/MSS) Adenocarcinoma
subtypes.description: pMMR/MSS tumors exhibit intact DNA mismatch repair protein function and stable microsatellite regions, representing the predominant molecular subtype of rectal cancer. These tumors are characterized by a relatively lower mutational burden and follow the chromosomal instability pathway of colorectal carcinogenesis. The NCCN Guidelines provide extensive treatment recommendations stratified by specific biomarkers within this subtype, including KRAS, NRAS, and BRAF mutation status, as well as HER2 overexpression/amplification status. For locally advanced disease (stage II–III), the recommended approach is total neoadjuvant therapy (TNT) with either induction or consolidation chemotherapy combined with radiation therapy, followed by total mesorectal excision (TME) [REC-6]. For metastatic disease, treatment follows a continuum-of-care paradigm with multiple lines of systemic therapy, including fluoropyrimidine-based chemotherapy, oxaliplatin, irinotecan, anti-VEGF biologics (bevacizumab, ziv-aflibercept, ramucirumab), and anti-EGFR biologics (cetuximab, panitumumab) for RAS/BRAF wild-type tumors [REC-F 2–4].
subtypes.frequency: Majority of rectal cancers; the document addresses pMMR/MSS as the predominant subtype with distinct treatment pathways from dMMR/MSI-H and POLE/POLD1-mutated tumors
key_associations: KRAS/NRAS mutations (exons 2, 3, 4)
BRAF V600E mutation
HER2 (ERBB2) overexpression/amplification
PI3K pathway alterations (PIK3CA, PIK3R1, PTEN)
Sensitivity to fluoropyrimidines, oxaliplatin, and irinotecan
Resistance to immune checkpoint inhibitor monotherapy
Requires multimodal treatment including surgery and often radiation

subtypes.name: Deficient Mismatch Repair/Microsatellite Instability-High (dMMR/MSI-H) Adenocarcinoma
subtypes.description: dMMR/MSI-H rectal cancers are characterized by loss of function in one or more mismatch repair proteins (MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, PMS2), leading to microsatellite instability and a high mutational burden. MMR proteins function as heterodimers (MLH1/PMS2 and MSH2/MSH6), so loss of expression of one protein typically results in loss of its heterodimer partner [REC-B 6]. Universal MMR or MSI testing is recommended in all newly diagnosed rectal cancer patients [REC-B 5]. Loss of MLH1 expression in the setting of BRAF V600E mutation would preclude Lynch syndrome diagnosis in the vast majority of patients, though approximately 1% of cancers with BRAF V600E mutations and loss of MLH1 still harbor Lynch syndrome [REC-B 5]. These tumors demonstrate exceptional sensitivity to immune checkpoint inhibitor immunotherapy, including PD-1 inhibitors (nivolumab, pembrolizumab, dostarlimab-gxly, cemiplimab-rwlc, and others) and combination PD-1/CTLA-4 blockade (ipilimumab + nivolumab) [REC-F 5]. For locally advanced nonmetastatic disease, neoadjuvant immunotherapy with checkpoint inhibitors has achieved complete clinical response rates enabling organ preservation without surgery, chemotherapy, or radiation in many patients [REC-14].
subtypes.frequency: Not specifically quantified for rectal cancer in this document; universal MMR/MSI testing is recommended for all newly diagnosed patients to identify this subtype and assess Lynch syndrome risk
key_associations: Lynch syndrome (hereditary nonpolyposis colorectal cancer)
MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, PMS2 protein loss by immunohistochemistry
High sensitivity to immune checkpoint inhibitors (PD-1/PD-L1/CTLA-4)
BRAF V600E mutation in setting of MLH1 loss generally excludes Lynch syndrome
Higher mutational burden than pMMR/MSS tumors
Potential for complete clinical response with immunotherapy alone
More favorable prognosis compared to pMMR/MSS disease

subtypes.name: POLE/POLD1 Ultra-Hypermutated Adenocarcinoma
subtypes.description: Tumors harboring somatic pathogenic variants in the exonuclease domains of POLE or POLD1 polymerase genes exhibit an ultra-hypermutated phenotype with TMB exceeding 50 mutations per megabase [REC-B 7]. POLE encodes the catalytic subunit of DNA polymerase epsilon, and POLD1 encodes the catalytic subunit of DNA polymerase delta, both involved in DNA replication proofreading. Loss of proofreading function leads to acquisition of numerous single nucleotide variants [REC-B 7]. Germline pathogenic variants predispose patients to polymerase proofreading-associated polyposis (PPAP) [REC-B 7]. These tumors demonstrate enhanced immune responses stimulated by numerous neoantigens, resulting in more favorable prognosis and excellent response to immune checkpoint inhibitor therapy [REC-B 7]. Multigene panel testing (MGPT) can identify POLE/POLD1 mutations and provides direct evidence of the ultra-hypermutated phenotype through TMB calculation [REC-B 7]. When both dMMR/MSI-H and BRAF V600E are positive, treatment should start with a checkpoint inhibitor if feasible [REC-F note].
subtypes.frequency: Somatic POLE mutations occur in approximately 2–8% of patients with predominantly MSS/pMMR colorectal cancers; somatic POLD1 mutations are extremely rare [REC-B 7]
key_associations: Ultra-hypermutated phenotype (TMB >50 mutations/Mb)
Polymerase proofreading-associated polyposis (PPAP) when germline
Favorable prognosis
Excellent response to immune checkpoint inhibitor therapy
Enhanced intratumoral immune responses due to numerous neoantigens
Predominantly MSS/pMMR by standard testing
Identifiable through multigene panel testing (MGPT)

subtypes.name: Mucinous Adenocarcinoma
subtypes.description: Mucinous adenocarcinoma is a histologic variant of rectal cancer characterized by the presence of extracellular mucin comprising more than 50% of the tumor volume. On pelvic MRI, mucinous tumors may demonstrate signal characteristics similar to fat on T2-weighted images, and T1-weighted images without contrast may be helpful for their identification and staging [REC-A 3 of 5]. This histologic subtype may have implications for imaging assessment and treatment response evaluation. The molecular profile and treatment approach generally follow the same guidelines as non-mucinous adenocarcinoma, contingent on MMR/MSI status and other biomarker testing results. For staging and restaging purposes, MRI assessment should account for the distinct signal characteristics of mucinous tumors.
subtypes.frequency: Not specifically quantified in this document
key_associations: Distinct MRI signal characteristics on T2-weighted images (similar to fat)
May require T1-weighted imaging for accurate assessment
Treatment guided by molecular subtype (pMMR/MSS or dMMR/MSI-H)
Considerations for accurate staging on pelvic MRI

tier_2_handbook.molecular_pathogenesis:
Rectal cancer molecular pathogenesis encompasses multiple genomic events, signaling pathways, and chromosomal abnormalities that define distinct biologic subtypes and guide therapeutic decision-making.

RAS/RAF Pathway: KRAS and NRAS mutations (affecting exons 2, 3, and 4) are key oncogenic drivers in colorectal cancer. Patients with any known KRAS or NRAS mutation should not receive anti-EGFR therapy with cetuximab or panitumumab, as these mutations confer resistance, unless given as part of a regimen targeting a KRAS G12C mutation [REC-B 5]. BRAF V600E mutation renders response to panitumumab or cetuximab highly unlikely [REC-B 5]. BRAF V600E mutation testing via immunohistochemistry (IHC) is an option [REC-B 5]. Encorafenib-based regimens (combined with cetuximab or panitumumab, with or without FOLFIRI or FOLFOX) are specifically indicated for BRAF V600E-mutant metastatic disease [REC-F 4]. Patients with BRAF mutations other than V600E may be considered for anti-EGFR therapy [REC-F footnote c].

Mismatch Repair/Microsatellite Instability: Deficient mismatch repair (dMMR) results from loss of function in MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, or PMS2 proteins. MMR proteins function as heterodimers (MLH1/PMS2 and MSH2/MSH6), so loss of one protein typically results in loss of its heterodimer partner [REC-B 6]. Loss of MLH1 expression in the presence of BRAF V600E mutation generally excludes Lynch syndrome diagnosis, though approximately 1% of BRAF V600E-mutant cancers with MLH1 loss still harbor Lynch syndrome [REC-B 5]. IHC testing looks at protein expression of all four MMR genes, and interpretation should report either "retained for all MMR proteins" or "loss of expression of (one or more of the MMR proteins)" [REC-B 6]. dMMR/MSI-H status is the primary determinant of eligibility for immune checkpoint inhibitor immunotherapy [REC-F 5]. Testing can be performed by IHC for protein expression, PCR for microsatellite instability, or as part of validated multigene panel testing [REC-B 5].

PI3K Pathway Alterations: Somatic PI3K pathway alterations include mutations in PIK3CA exons 9 and 20, other PIK3CA mutations, PIK3R1 mutations, PTEN mutations, and deep deletions of PTEN [REC-2 footnote m]. These alterations are clinically significant for stage II–III disease, as PIK3CA-mutant tumors benefit from adjuvant aspirin therapy (100–162 mg PO daily for 3 years after surgery). The ALASCCA trial reported hazard ratios for time to recurrence of 0.49 (95% CI, 0.24–0.98; P = .044) for PIK3CA exon 9/20 mutations and 0.42 (95% CI, 0.21–0.83; P = .013) for other PI3K pathway alterations with aspirin versus placebo [MS-30]. Molecular profiling to include somatic PI3K pathway alterations should be performed on all stage II and III colorectal cancer tumors [REC-3A].

POLE/POLD1 Proofreading Deficiency: Pathogenic variants in the exonuclease domains of POLE and POLD1 result in loss of DNA replication proofreading function, leading to ultra-hypermutated tumors with TMB exceeding 50 mutations per megabase [REC-B 7]. Somatic POLE mutations occur in approximately 2–8% of predominantly MSS/pMMR colorectal cancers, while somatic POLD1 mutations are extremely rare [REC-B 7]. Germline pathogenic variants predispose patients to polymerase proofreading-associated polyposis (PPAP) [REC-B 7]. These tumors exhibit enhanced immune responses due to numerous neoantigens and demonstrate favorable prognosis with excellent response to immune checkpoint inhibitor therapy [REC-B 7].

NTRK Gene Fusions: NTRK 1/2/3 gene fusions are extremely rare in colorectal cancers, occurring in approximately 0.35% of cases (8 of 2314 CRCs in one cohort) [REC-B 6]. These fusions are confined to tumors that are wild-type for KRAS, NRAS, and BRAF. Seven of eight CRCs harboring NTRK fusions were found in the dMMR/MSI-H subset [REC-B 6]. TRK inhibitors (larotrectinib, entrectinib, repotrectinib) demonstrate activity exclusively in tumors with NTRK gene fusions, not point mutations [REC-B 6]. RNA-based next-generation sequencing appears to be the optimal detection method, as it provides direct evidence of functional transcription [REC-B 6].

RET Gene Fusions: Activating RET gene fusions involving the C-terminal kinase domain lead to constitutive upregulation of RET kinase activity, promoting cell proliferation and survival through downstream MAPK and PI3K signaling pathways [REC-B 7]. The most common fusion partners reported in CRC include KIF5B, CCDC6, and NCOA4 [REC-B 7]. Selpercatinib is FDA-approved for RET fusion-positive solid tumors [REC-B 7]. Caution should be used in interpreting RET gene fusion results from ctDNA/RNA assays following targeted therapy with EGFR inhibitors [REC-B 7].

HER2 (ERBB2) Overexpression/Amplification: HER2 positivity is defined as IHC 3+ staining in >50% of tumor cells (intense membrane staining that can be circumferential, basolateral, or lateral) or amplification by FISH (HER2:CEP17 ratio ≥2 in >50% of cells) [REC-B 6]. Anti-HER2 therapies including pertuzumab or tucatinib combined with trastuzumab are indicated for HER2-overexpressing tumors that are KRAS/NRAS/BRAF wild-type [REC-F]. Fam-trastuzumab deruxtecan-nxki is indicated for HER2-overexpressing tumors with IHC 3+ [REC-F].

Tumor Budding: Defined as single cells or clusters of ≤4 neoplastic cells at the advancing edge of invasive carcinoma, tumor budding is graded as low (0–4 buds), intermediate (5–9 buds), or high (≥10 buds) per the International Tumor Budding Consensus Conference [REC-B 3]. High-grade tumor budding is an independent adverse prognostic factor for stage II colon cancer and is associated with increased risk of lymph node metastasis in pT1 cancers [REC-B 3]. A meta-analysis showed high-grade tumor budding increased the risk of poor outcomes in rectal cancer after neoadjuvant therapy (poor 5-year DFS, P < .00001; poor 5-year OS, P = .003; local recurrence, P = .007; and distant metastasis, P < .001) [MS-6].

epidemiology.incidence: Colorectal cancer (CRC) is the fourth most frequently diagnosed cancer and the second leading cause of cancer death in the United States [MS-2]. In 2025, an estimated 46,950 new cases of rectal cancer will occur in the United States (27,950 cases in males; 19,000 cases in females) [MS-2]. Approximately one-third of all colorectal cancers occur in the rectum [MS-2]. The overall age-adjusted incidence of colorectal cancer per 100,000 people decreased from 60.5 in 1976 to 46.4 in 2005 and, more recently, to 38.7 in 2016 [MS-2]. This decreasing overall trend reflects the impact of screening, polypectomy, and improved treatment, though significant increases are now noted in younger populations.
epidemiology.mortality: In 2025, it is estimated that 52,900 people will die from rectal and colon cancers combined in the United States [MS-2]. Mortality from colorectal cancer has been decreasing for decades (since 1947 in females and since 1980 in males) and is currently down by more than 50% from peak mortality rates [MS-2]. The decline in mortality is attributed to cancer prevention, earlier diagnoses through screening, and improved treatment modalities [MS-2]. More than half of patients who die of CRC have liver metastases at autopsy, with metastatic liver disease as the cause of death in most patients [MS-33].
epidemiology.trends: Recent data show continued rapid declines in colorectal cancer incidence among those aged 65 years and older, with a decrease of 3.3% annually between 2011 and 2016 [MS-2]. Conversely, incidence has increased among those younger than 65 years, with a 1% annual increase in those aged 50 to 64 years and a 2% annual increase in those younger than 50 years [MS-2]. Mortality trends mirror these age-dependent patterns: declining by 3% annually for those 65 years and older, compared to a 0.6% annual decline for individuals aged 50 to 64 years and a 1.3% annual increase for individuals younger than 50 years [MS-2]. A retrospective cohort study of the SEER CRC registry estimated that incidence rates for rectal cancer will increase by 124.2% for patients aged 20 to 34 years by 2030 [MS-2]. CRC that occurs in patients younger than 45 years may be clinicopathologically and genetically different from CRC in older adults, though this has not been confirmed broadly [MS-2].
epidemiology.demographics: Colorectal cancer incidence and mortality rates vary by race and ethnicity, with the highest rates in non-Hispanic Black individuals and the lowest in Asian Americans/Pacific Islanders [MS-2]. The magnitude of disparity in mortality rates is double that of incidence rates [MS-2]. Reasons for these racial disparities include differences in risk factor prevalence, access to health care and other social determinants of health, comorbidities, and tumor characteristics [MS-2]. Approximately 20% of cases of colorectal cancer are associated with familial clustering, and first-degree relatives of patients with colorectal adenomas or invasive CRC are at increased risk [MS-3]. Metastatic disease develops in 50%–60% of patients diagnosed with CRC, with 20%–34% presenting with synchronous liver metastases at diagnosis [MS-33]. Approximately 17% of patients with metastatic CRC have peritoneal carcinomatosis, with 2% having the peritoneum as the only site of metastasis [MS-34]. Lung metastases occur in approximately 4%–9% of patients with colon and rectal cancer [MS-33].
epidemiology.notes: The NCCN Rectal Cancer Panel recognizes the importance of clinical trials and encourages participation when applicable and available, with trials designed to maximize inclusiveness and broad representative enrollment. The guidelines overlap considerably with the NCCN Guidelines for Colon Cancer, especially in the treatment of metastatic disease [MS-2].

risk_factors.factor: Family history of colorectal cancer or adenomatous polyps
risk_factors.detail: Approximately 20% of cases of colorectal cancer are associated with familial clustering. First-degree relatives of patients with colorectal adenomas or invasive CRC are at increased risk for colorectal cancer [MS-3]. The presence of a first-degree relative with CRC confers increased lifetime risk. All patients with rectal cancer should be counseled regarding family history [REC-1 footnote a].

Lynch syndrome is caused by germline pathogenic variants in mismatch repair genes (MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, PMS2) and confers significantly elevated lifetime risk of colorectal cancer. Universal MMR or MSI testing is recommended in all newly diagnosed rectal cancer patients to identify potential Lynch syndrome [REC-B 5]. Loss of MLH1 expression in the setting of BRAF V600E mutation would preclude Lynch syndrome diagnosis in the vast majority of patients [REC-B 5]. The NCCN Guidelines for Genetic/Familial High-Risk Assessment: Colorectal, Endometrial, and Gastric provide detailed management recommendations [REC-1 footnote a].

risk_factors.factor: Familial adenomatous polyposis (FAP) and attenuated FAP
risk_factors.detail: FAP is characterized by development of hundreds to thousands of colorectal adenomatous polyps, with near-universal progression to colorectal cancer if untreated. Attenuated FAP presents with fewer polyps (typically 10–99) and later age of onset. All patients with rectal cancer should be counseled for family history, and those with suspected FAP or attenuated FAP should be referred for genetic risk assessment [REC-1 footnote a].

Colorectal cancer incidence traditionally increases with age, though rising incidence has been documented in younger populations. The estimated incidence rate for rectal cancer in patients aged 20 to 34 years is projected to increase by 124.2% by 2030 [MS-2]. CRC that occurs in patients younger than 45 years may be clinicopathologically and genetically different from CRC in adults 45 years and older [MS-2]. The NCCN Guidelines recommend tools to aid optimal assessment and care of older adults with cancer [REC-2 footnote j].

Colorectal cancer incidence and mortality rates vary by race and ethnicity, with the highest rates in non-Hispanic Black individuals and the lowest in Asian Americans/Pacific Islanders [MS-2]. The magnitude of disparity in mortality rates is double that of incidence rates. Reasons include differences in risk factor prevalence, access to health care, social determinants of health, comorbidities, and tumor characteristics [MS-2].

risk_factors.factor: Somatic PI3K pathway alterations (treatment risk modifier)
risk_factors.detail: Tumors harboring somatic PI3K pathway alterations (PIK3CA exon 9 and 20 mutations, other PIK3CA mutations, PIK3R1 mutations, PTEN mutations, or deep deletions of PTEN) modify treatment recommendations. For stage II–III disease with these alterations, adjuvant aspirin is recommended, based on data showing reduced recurrence [REC-3A]. Molecular profiling to include PI3K pathway alterations should be done on all stage II and III CRC tumors [REC-3A].
risk_factors.strength: Moderate (emerging evidence from prospective randomized trials)

risk_factors.detail: The NCCN Rectal Cancer Guidelines reference the NCCN Guidelines for Colon Cancer for detailed discussion of risk factors including inflammatory bowel disease. Chronic colonic inflammation is an established risk factor for colorectal carcinogenesis. Patients with long-standing ulcerative colitis or Crohn's colitis require enhanced surveillance.
risk_factors.strength: Strong (established risk factor, details in NCCN Colon Cancer guidelines)

protective_factors: Cancer screening and early detection through polypectomy: The improvements in colorectal cancer incidence and mortality are thought to be a result of cancer prevention and earlier diagnoses through screening, as well as improved treatment modalities [MS-2]. Colonoscopy-based screening enables detection and removal of precancerous adenomatous polyps before malignant transformation, contributing to the overall decline in colorectal cancer incidence from 60.5 per 100,000 in 1976 to 38.7 per 100,000 in 2016 [MS-2].

Adjuvant aspirin for PIK3CA-mutated stage II–III disease: For stage II and III colorectal cancer tumors harboring somatic PI3K pathway alterations, daily aspirin (100–162 mg PO) for 3 years after recovery from surgery is recommended [REC-3A]. The ALASCCA trial reported hazard ratios for time to recurrence of 0.49 (95% CI, 0.24–0.98; P = .044) in Group A (PIK3CA exon 9/20 mutations) and 0.42 (95% CI, 0.21–0.83; P = .013) in Group B (other PI3K pathway alterations) with aspirin versus placebo [MS-30]. Aspirin should not be initiated until after recovery from surgery and can be given concurrently with adjuvant chemotherapy [REC-3A footnote m].

Neoadjuvant checkpoint inhibitor immunotherapy for dMMR/MSI-H or POLE/POLD1-mutated locally advanced disease: For stage II–III dMMR/MSI-H or POLE/POLD1-mutated rectal cancer, neoadjuvant immunotherapy with PD-1 inhibitors (dostarlimab-gxly, nivolumab, or pembrolizumab) is the preferred approach [REC-14]. Complete clinical response has been achieved in the majority of patients, enabling organ preservation without surgery, radiation, or chemotherapy [REC-14].

clinical_features.typical_presentation: Rectal cancer commonly presents with changes in bowel habits including increased frequency, narrower stools, or a feeling of incomplete evacuation. Patients may report rectal bleeding (hematochezia), tenesmus (a constant feeling of needing to pass stools), abdominal or pelvic pain, and in advanced cases, bowel obstruction or perforation. Approximately 20% to 34% of patients with colorectal cancer present with synchronous liver metastases, and lung metastases occur in approximately 4% to 9% of patients. The rectum is defined as lying below a virtual line from the sacral promontory to the upper edge of the symphysis as determined by MRI [1,2]. Early-stage rectal cancers may be detected incidentally during screening colonoscopy or as malignant polyps (pT1 invasive adenocarcinomas). Malignant polyps are defined as those harboring cancer invading through the muscularis mucosae into the submucosa [3].

symptoms.name: Rectal bleeding
symptoms.detail: Hematochezia or occult blood in stool; one of the most common presenting symptoms

symptoms.name: Change in bowel habits
symptoms.detail: Increased frequency, narrower stools, diarrhea alternating with constipation

symptoms.name: Tenesmus
symptoms.detail: Constant feeling of incomplete evacuation or urgency to defecate

symptoms.name: Abdominal or pelvic pain
symptoms.detail: May be crampy or persistent; more common with advanced or obstructing tumors

symptoms.name: Bowel obstruction
symptoms.detail: Partial or complete obstruction from advanced tumor; can present as acute emergency

symptoms.name: Incidental detection on colonoscopy
symptoms.detail: Malignant polyps detected during screening or diagnostic colonoscopy; polyps may be pedunculated or sessile

symptoms.name: Perforation
symptoms.detail: Spontaneous or secondary to tumor growth; medical emergency

signs.name: Palpable rectal mass
signs.detail: Identified on digital rectal examination (DRE); critical to document tumor size, distance from anal verge, orientation within rectal lumen, degree of circumferential involvement, extent of fixation, and sphincter involvement

signs.name: Lymphadenopathy
signs.detail: Enlarged pelvic or inguinal lymph nodes on examination or imaging may indicate metastatic disease

signs.name: Fecal occult blood
signs.detail: Detectable on DRE or fecal immunochemical test

signs.name: Obstructing lesion
signs.detail: May present with distended abdomen, tympany, or signs of bowel obstruction

atypical_or_missed: Asymptomatic early-stage cancers detected only on screening colonoscopy
Malignant polyps (pT1) may not cause symptoms and are found incidentally
Sessile malignant polyps may be confused with benign polyps on initial endoscopy
Tumors high in the rectum may mimic colon cancer symptoms and be misidentified anatomically
Left-sided colon cancers may present with iron deficiency anemia without obvious bleeding
Young adults (<50 years) with rectal cancer may have delayed diagnosis due to lower clinical suspicion; CRC incidence in patients <50 years is increasing by approximately 2% annually

red_flags: Rectal bleeding in patients over 45 years or with family history of colorectal cancer
New change in bowel habits persisting more than several weeks
Tenemus or feeling of incomplete evacuation
Bowel obstruction symptoms (abdominal distension, vomiting, inability to pass flatus)
Iron deficiency anemia without an identified cause
Family history suggestive of Lynch syndrome (hereditary nonpolyposis CRC), familial adenomatous polyposis (FAP), or attenuated FAP
New-onset symptoms in patients under 50 years of age, given rising incidence in younger populations
Perforation of a rectal mass presenting as acute abdomen
Serial CEA elevation during surveillance suggesting recurrence

diagnostic.rationale: Essential for all patients with rectal cancer to evaluate for synchronous lesions, other polyps, or other pathologic conditions of the colon and rectum
diagnostic.key_findings: Tumor location, size, degree of circumferential involvement, presence of synchronous polyps or cancers

diagnostic.rationale: Useful in determining distance of cancer from anal verge and length of tumor; may be performed at time of colonoscopy or within 2 weeks if appropriate
diagnostic.key_findings: Distance from anal verge, tumor length, orientation within rectal lumen

diagnostic.rationale: Confirm presence of invasive cancer (pT1); pTis has no biological potential to metastasize; careful pathology review for evidence of invasion through muscularis mucosa
diagnostic.key_findings: Histologic confirmation of adenocarcinoma, depth of invasion, grade of tumor

diagnostic.rationale: Critical component of clinical assessment; should be performed by treating surgeon independently
diagnostic.key_findings: Tumor size, distance from anal verge and anorectal ring, degree of fixation, sphincter involvement and tone, tumor orientation

staging.test: Pelvis MRI with or without contrast
staging.rationale: Preferred imaging for local staging of primary rectal tumor; assess T and N stage; predict CRM status; minimum requirement 1.5 T with external phased array coil; endorectal coils expressly NOT recommended
staging.indication: All rectal cancer patients; assess tumor extent, mesorectal fascia involvement, lymph node status, extramural vascular invasion (EMVI)

staging.rationale: Alternative to MRI when MRI is contraindicated (e.g., pacemaker) or inconclusive, or for superficial (T1) lesions; limited for high or bulky tumors
staging.indication: T1 tumors, MRI contraindicated, or inconclusive MRI

staging.rationale: Assess for distant metastatic disease to lungs, thoracic lymph nodes
staging.indication: All patients undergoing staging for rectal cancer

staging.test: Abdominal CT or MRI with IV contrast
staging.rationale: Assess for liver metastases, peritoneal cavity, abdominal lymph nodes; MRI with hepatobiliary agent preferred for liver-directed therapy planning
staging.indication: All patients; consider MRI of liver for potentially resectable disease

staging.rationale: Not routinely indicated for staging; may be considered for potentially surgically curable M1 disease in selected cases; does not supplant contrast-enhanced CT or MRI; may be necessary for equivocal findings
staging.indication: Potentially surgically curable M1 disease; image-guided liver-directed therapy planning; equivocal CT/MRI findings

biomarkers.test: Mismatch repair (MMR) or microsatellite instability (MSI) testing
biomarkers.rationale: Universal MMR or MSI testing recommended in ALL newly diagnosed rectal cancer patients; determines eligibility for checkpoint inhibitor immunotherapy; identifies Lynch syndrome

biomarkers.test: KRAS/NRAS and BRAF V600E mutation testing
biomarkers.rationale: Required for all patients with metastatic CRC; BRAF V600E mutation makes response to panitumumab or cetuximab highly unlikely; KRAS/NRAS mutations preclude EGFR inhibitor use
biomarkers.when: At initial diagnosis of metastatic disease

biomarkers.rationale: Required for patients with metastatic disease to determine eligibility for anti-HER2 therapy (trastuzumab-based regimens, fam-trastuzumab deruxtecan-nxki)
biomarkers.when: At initial diagnosis of metastatic disease

biomarkers.test: Molecular profiling to include somatic PI3K pathway alterations
biomarkers.rationale: Somatic PI3K pathway alterations include mutations in PIK3CA exon 9 and 20, other PIK3CA, PIK3R1, and PTEN mutations, and deep deletions of PTEN; if present, aspirin 100-162 mg PO daily for 3 years after recovery from surgery is recommended for stage II and III
biomarkers.when: All stage II and III CRC tumors

biomarkers.rationale: Should be conducted as part of MGPT to identify rare and actionable mutations and fusions such as POLE/POLD1, RET, and NTRK 1/2/3; may be tissue- or blood-based
Baseline and surveillance marker; elevated CEA may indicate metastatic disease; sensitivity 68% (95% CI, 53%-79%) and specificity 97% (95% CI, 90%-99%) at cutoff of 10 ng/mL
biomarkers.when: Baseline and during surveillance every 3-6 months for 2 years, then every 6 months for total of 5 years

biomarkers.test: Complete blood count (CBC) and chemistry profile
biomarkers.rationale: Baseline laboratory assessment prior to treatment
FDA Black Box warning to test patients for genetic variants of DPYD prior to initiating capecitabine unless immediate treatment is necessary

do_not_miss: MMR/MSI testing for all newly diagnosed rectal cancer patients
Pelvis MRI for local staging (T and N stage, mesorectal fascia, EMVI)
Chest and abdominal imaging for distant metastases
Multidisciplinary team evaluation including formal surgical evaluation
Family history assessment for Lynch syndrome, FAP, and attenuated FAP
PI3K pathway mutation testing for stage II-III tumors
Fertility risk discussion/counseling in appropriate patients prior to treatment
avoid: Pelvic CT for T and N staging (poor sensitivity and specificity compared to MRI)
Routine FDG-PET/CT for initial staging (not routinely indicated; does not supplant contrast-enhanced CT or MRI)
Routine FDG-PET/CT during surveillance (usually not recommended)
ctDNA for treatment decision-making outside of clinical trials (insufficient evidence; de-escalation or treatment changes not recommended based on ctDNA results)
Endorectal coils for pelvic MRI (expressly NOT recommended)
T1-weighted contrast-enhanced sequences for staging (no conclusive evidence for staging or restaging value at this time)

t_stages.description: Carcinoma in situ, intramucosal carcinoma (involvement of lamina propria with no extension through muscularis mucosae)
Tumor invades the submucosa (through the muscularis mucosae but not into the muscularis propria)
Tumor invades through the muscularis propria into pericolorectal tissues
Tumor invades the visceral peritoneum or invades or adheres to adjacent organ or structure
Tumor invades through the visceral peritoneum (including gross perforation of the bowel through tumor and continuous invasion through areas of inflammation to the surface of the visceral peritoneum)
Tumor directly invades or adheres to adjacent organs or structures

n_stages.description: Regional lymph nodes cannot be assessed
One to three regional lymph nodes are positive (tumor in lymph nodes measuring ≥0.2 mm), or any number of tumor deposits are present and all identifiable lymph nodes are negative
One regional lymph node is positive
Two or three regional lymph nodes are positive
No regional lymph nodes are positive, but there are tumor deposits in the subserosa, mesentery, or nonperitonealized pericolic, or perirectal/mesorectal tissues
Four or more regional lymph nodes are positive
Four to six regional lymph nodes are positive
Seven or more regional lymph nodes are positive

m_stages.description: No distant metastasis by imaging, etc.; no evidence of tumor in distant sites or organs
Metastasis to one or more distant sites or organs or peritoneal metastasis is identified
Metastasis to one site or organ is identified without peritoneal metastasis
Metastasis to two or more sites or organs is identified without peritoneal metastasis
Metastasis to the peritoneal surface is identified alone or with other site or organ metastases

stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Carcinoma in situ; confined to mucosa; no metastatic potential
Tumor invades submucosa or muscularis propria; no nodal involvement
Tumor penetrates through muscularis propria into pericolorectal tissues; node-negative
Tumor penetrates through visceral peritoneum; node-negative
Tumor directly invades or adheres to adjacent organs/structures; node-negative

stage_groups.criteria: T1-T2, N1/N1c, M0 OR T1, N2a, M0
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Low T-stage with regional nodal involvement

stage_groups.criteria: T3-T4a, N1/N1c, M0 OR T2-T3, N2a, M0 OR T1-T2, N2b, M0
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Higher T-stage with regional nodal involvement or lower T-stage with extensive nodal involvement

stage_groups.criteria: T4a, N2a, M0 OR T3-T4a, N2b, M0 OR T4b, N1-N2, M0
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Advanced local disease with extensive nodal involvement
Metastasis to one site or organ without peritoneal metastasis
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Palliative with potential for cure in select resectable cases

stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Metastasis to two or more sites or organs without peritoneal metastasis
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Palliative with potential for cure in select resectable cases

stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Peritoneal metastasis with or without blood-borne metastasis

staging_pearls: T stage has more prognostic value than N stage in rectal cancer; patients with stage IIIA (T1-T2) have longer rectal cancer-specific survival than patients with stage IIA (T3), IIB (T4a), and IIC (T4b) rectal cancer

Tumor deposits (N1c) are irregular discrete tumor deposits in perirectal fat away from the leading edge of the tumor showing no evidence of residual lymph node tissue; they are associated with reduced DFS and OS
A positive circumferential resection margin (CRM) is defined as tumor within 1 mm from the resected margin and is a strong predictor of local recurrence and OS
Patients with peritoneal carcinomatosis (M1c) have shorter PFS and OS than those without peritoneal involvement
The prefixes 'p' and 'yp' denote pathologic staging and pathologic staging following neoadjuvant therapy respectively

For stage II (pN0) disease, if <12 lymph nodes are initially identified, the pathologist should resubmit more tissue; lymph node retrieval is significantly reduced after neoadjuvant therapy (mean 13 vs 19, P < .05)
Isolated tumor clusters (ITC) measuring <0.2 mm are classified as N0 in the AJCC 8th edition staging system, while micrometastatic deposits measuring 0.2-2.0 mm are considered positive lymph nodes
Perineural invasion (PNI) is an independent prognostic factor; for stage II rectal cancer, those with PNI have significantly worse 5-year DFS compared to those without PNI (29% vs. 82%; P = .0005)
Rectal cancer has higher pelvic recurrence risk than colon cancer due to close proximity to pelvic structures, absence of serosa, and technical difficulties with wide surgical margins

prognosis.overall: Colorectal cancer is the fourth most frequently diagnosed cancer and the second leading cause of cancer death in the United States. Approximately a third of colorectal cancers occur in the rectum. In 2025, an estimated 46,950 new cases of rectal cancer are expected in the US, with 52,900 deaths from rectal and colon cancers combined. Mortality rates have decreased by >50% from peak rates due to improved screening and treatment. [1]

by_stage.context: Generally considered localized disease with a favorable prognosis when treated with surgery.
Prognosis varies based on specific pathologic features (e.g., T4 stage, lymphovascular invasion, perineural invasion).
Node-positive disease; prognosis is inversely correlated with the extent of nodal involvement (N1 vs. N2).

by_stage.stage: Stage IV (Any T, Any N, M1)
by_stage.context: Metastatic disease. Prognosis depends on the extent of metastases (M1a, M1b, M1c) and resectability. Patients with peritoneal metastases (M1c) have a shorter progression-free and overall survival than those without peritoneal involvement. [2]

prognostic_factors: Circumferential resection margin (CRM) status (involved or threatened CRM defined as tumor within 1 mm of the resected margin)
Perineural invasion (PNI) - associated with significantly worse prognosis; an independent prognostic factor for cancer-specific, overall, and disease-free survival
Tumor deposits (irregular discrete tumor deposits in perirectal fat without residual lymph node tissue) - associated with reduced disease-free and overall survival
Tumor budding (single cells or clusters of ≤4 cells at the invasive edge) - high-grade budding is an adverse prognostic factor and may inform treatment decisions
Response to neoadjuvant therapy (tumor regression grade, ypT/ypN stage)
Microsatellite instability (MSI) or mismatch repair (MMR) status - dMMR/MSI-H tumors have a more favorable prognosis
POLE/POLD1 mutations - associated with an ultra-hypermutated phenotype (TMB >50 mut/Mb) and a more favorable prognosis, likely due to enhanced immune response
prognosis.prognostic_context: Prognosis in rectal cancer is multifactorial and heavily influenced by stage at diagnosis, pathologic risk factors, and response to neoadjuvant therapy. The introduction of total neoadjuvant therapy (TNT) and immunotherapy for dMMR/MSI-H tumors has altered prognostic expectations for specific subgroups, with high rates of clinical complete response and organ preservation.

disease_related.management: May require emergent surgical intervention, diverting ostomy, or stenting for palliation. Bevacizumab use in the setting of colon/rectal stents may increase the risk of perforation.
Supportive care including iron supplementation, transfusion as needed, and nutritional support.

disease_related.complication: Local invasion causing fistulae or organ dysfunction
disease_related.management: Multidisciplinary management including palliative radiation, chemotherapy, or surgery.

chemotherapy: Peripheral sensory neuropathy (oxaliplatin) - may be chronic and cumulative; discontinuation should be considered after 3-4 months or for unacceptable neurotoxicity. Duloxetine may be considered for painful neuropathy.
immunotherapy: Immune-related adverse events (irAEs) - management per NCCN Guidelines for Management of Immune Checkpoint Inhibitor-Related Toxicities.
Colitis, pneumonitis, hepatitis, endocrinopathies, dermatologic reactions.
targeted_therapy: Hypertension, proteinuria, impaired wound healing (bevacizumab) - should be held for at least 28 days prior to elective surgery.
Acneiform rash, hypomagnesemia (EGFR inhibitors: cetuximab, panitumumab)
Increased risk of pulmonary hemorrhage, especially for ultracentral tumors, with combination of bevacizumab and SBRT.
surgery: Bowel dysfunction (anterior resection syndrome - urgency, frequency, clustering)
Stoma-related complications (if permanent colostomy after APR)
radiation: Acute: radiation dermatitis, cystitis, proctitis, diarrhea, fatigue
Late: bowel obstruction, fistula, sexual dysfunction, pelvic fractures/decreased bone density, vaginal stenosis
Small bowel toxicity (dose constraints: Dmax 55 Gy, V45 Gy ≤150 cc for individual loops)

supportive_care.overview: Supportive care is integral throughout the treatment of rectal cancer, addressing physical, psychological, and social needs. It includes symptom management, nutritional support, and prevention/treatment of therapy-related toxicities. A multidisciplinary team approach is essential.
supportive_care.nutritional_support: Assessment by a registered dietitian is recommended. Management may include oral nutritional supplements, dietary modification, and parenteral nutrition if necessary. Pelvic radiation can cause malabsorption and diarrhea; dietary manipulation and bulk-forming agents may help.
supportive_care.anti_emetic_protocol: Follow anti-emetic guidelines based on the emetogenic potential of the chemotherapy regimen. 5-HT3 receptor antagonists and dexamethasone are commonly used. NK1 receptor antagonists may be added for highly emetogenic regimens.
supportive_care.gcsf_guidance: Primary prophylaxis with granulocyte colony-stimulating factor (G-CSF) should be considered for regimens with a >20% risk of febrile neutropenia. Per NCCN guidelines for myeloid growth factors.
supportive_care.vte_prophylaxis: Ambulation and mechanical prophylaxis are standard. Pharmacologic prophylaxis should be considered for high-risk patients, especially post-surgery, but careful risk-benefit assessment is needed given bleeding risk.
supportive_care.pain_management: A multimodal approach is recommended. For oxaliplatin-induced neuropathy, duloxetine is recommended for painful neuropathy (not effective for numbness, tingling, or cold sensitivity). Pregabalin or gabapentin are not recommended. Referral to a pain management specialist for refractory cases.
supportive_care.psychosocial_support: Screen for distress and provide resources (NCCN Guidelines for Distress Management). Address concerns about body image, sexuality, and fertility. Refer to social work, support groups, and survivorship programs as needed.
supportive_care.dental_care: Good oral hygiene is important, especially during chemotherapy. A dental evaluation prior to treatment is recommended for patients requiring oral bisphosphonates or with poor dental health.

follow_up.post_curative_treatment: Follow-up is designed to monitor for recurrence, detect new metachronous neoplasms, and manage long-term sequelae. Intensity is based on stage and risk of recurrence. After curative-intent treatment, 95% of recurrences occur within the first 5 years. [3, 4]
follow_up.surveillance_rationale: Early detection of potentially resectable recurrent disease may allow for curative-intent salvage surgery. Surveillance also identifies metachronous polyps and second primary cancers at a pre-invasive stage. More intensive surveillance programs have been shown to detect recurrences earlier, but the impact on overall survival remains debated. [5, 6]
late_effects_screening: Bowel function changes (chronic diarrhea, incontinence, urgency, clustering) - assess and manage with anti-diarrheal agents, bulk-forming agents, diet modification, pelvic floor rehabilitation.
Urogenital dysfunction (sexual dysfunction, erectile dysfunction, urinary incontinence, frequency, urgency) - screen and refer to urology/gynecology as needed.
Oxaliplatin-induced peripheral neuropathy - monitor symptoms; duloxetine for painful neuropathy.
Fatigue, insomnia, cognitive dysfunction - address with lifestyle modifications and referral if persistent.
Psychosocial distress - screen and provide support.
Potential for pelvic fractures/decreased bone density after pelvic radiation - consider bone density monitoring.
follow_up.recurrence_patterns: Most recurrences occur within the first 2-3 years. Local recurrence rates have been reduced with TME and neoadjuvant therapy. Distant metastases most commonly involve the liver and lungs. The pattern of recurrence (isolated local vs. distant) dictates further treatment options.

clinical_follow_up_schedule: Low-risk polyps removed by polypectomy: Physical exam and proctoscopy every 3-6 months for 2 years. Colonoscopy at 1 year, then as per adenoma findings.
Transanal local excision only: Proctoscopy (with EUS or MRI) every 3-6 months for 2 years, then every 6 months for 5 years. Colonoscopy at 1 year.
Stage I with full surgical staging: Colonoscopy at 1 year.

Stage II-IV: History and physical exam every 3-6 months for 2 years, then every 6 months for 5 years. CEA every 3-6 months for 2 years, then every 6 months for 5 years (if candidate for curative-intent metastasectomy). Colonoscopy at 1 year, then as per adenoma findings.
imaging_strategy: Stage II-III: Chest/abdomen/pelvis CT every 6-12 months for 5 years (category 2B for frequency <12 months).
Stage IV: C/A/P CT every 3-6 months for 2 years (category 2B for frequency <6 months), then every 6-12 months for 5 years.
After nonoperative management (watch-and-wait): MRI rectum every 6 months for up to 3 years. CT chest/abdomen every 6-12 months for 5 years.
FDG-PET/CT is usually not recommended for routine surveillance, but may be considered for equivocal findings or evaluation of isolated CEA elevation.
laboratory_monitoring: CEA testing as per schedule above.
Routine blood counts and chemistry profile as needed for monitoring treatment effects and comorbidities.
ctDNA is not recommended for routine surveillance or treatment de-escalation outside of clinical trials.
supportive_follow_up: Colonoscopy: At 1 year post-resection, then at 3 years, then every 5 years unless advanced adenoma found (repeat in 1 year).
Screening for second primary cancers per age-appropriate guidelines.
Referral to ostomy support group if applicable.

special_situations.situation: dMMR/MSI-H or POLE/POLD1 mutation with ultra-hypermutated phenotype (TMB >50 mut/Mb)
special_situations.key_message: These tumors have a distinct prognosis and treatment paradigm, often responding well to immune checkpoint inhibitors.
special_situations.management: Preferred treatment for stage II-III disease is neoadjuvant checkpoint inhibitor immunotherapy (dostarlimab, nivolumab, or pembrolizumab) for up to 6 months, potentially allowing for organ preservation (nonoperative management). For metastatic disease, checkpoint inhibitors are preferred first-line therapy.
special_situations.evidence: Phase II trials (e.g., dostarlimab, pembrolizumab) have shown high clinical complete response rates (approaching 100%) in dMMR/MSI-H locally advanced rectal cancer, enabling a watch-and-wait approach. [7, 8]

special_situations.key_message: Treatment options depend on tumor risk stratification (favorable vs. high-risk) and patient factors.
special_situations.management: Favorable-risk: Consider endoscopic submucosal dissection (ESD), transanal local excision, or transabdominal resection. High-risk: Transabdominal resection is preferred. After local excision, if adverse features (positive margins, LVI, sm3 invasion) are found, completion transabdominal resection is recommended.
special_situations.evidence: ESD and transanal local excision are appropriate for selected T1 tumors with favorable histology. Transabdominal resection provides accurate nodal staging and is recommended for high-risk features.

special_situations.key_message: Treatment intent may be curative with a multidisciplinary approach.
special_situations.management: For pMMR/MSS tumors: Total neoadjuvant therapy (TNT) with chemotherapy and RT, followed by staged or synchronous resection of primary and metastases. For dMMR/MSI-H tumors: Upfront checkpoint inhibitor immunotherapy is preferred, followed by RT and/or surgery if needed. Local therapies for oligometastases include resection, ablation, or SBRT.
special_situations.evidence: TNT (e.g., RAPIDO trial) has improved disease-free survival in locally advanced rectal cancer. [9] Resection of colorectal liver metastases can achieve long-term survival in selected patients. [10]

special_situations.key_message: Systemic therapy is the mainstay, with the goal of converting to resectability in select cases.
special_situations.management: Continuum of care based on biomarker status, prior therapy, and performance status. For pMMR/MSS: Intensive first-line regimens (FOLFOX, CAPEOX, FOLFIRI, FOLFIRINOX) with or without biologic therapy. For dMMR/MSI-H: Checkpoint inhibitors. Re-evaluate for resection every 2 months if conversion is a goal.
special_situations.evidence: The concept of conversion therapy is supported by studies showing that selected patients with initially unresectable liver metastases can undergo curative resection after systemic therapy. [11]

special_situations.key_message: Multidisciplinary evaluation is crucial to determine resectability and optimal treatment sequence.
special_situations.management: Potentially resectable isolated pelvic/anastomotic recurrence (pMMR/MSS): Consider neoadjuvant therapy (chemotherapy before or after chemoRT) followed by resection. IORT should be considered. For dMMR/MSI-H: Checkpoint inhibitor immunotherapy (if not previously received) or resection followed by RT.
special_situations.evidence: Re-resection for isolated local recurrence can be curative in selected patients, with 5-year overall survival rates around 15.6% in population-based data. [12]

clinical_pearls: Multidisciplinary team evaluation is essential for all rectal cancer patients, particularly for treatment planning in locally advanced and metastatic disease.
The rectum is defined as the segment of large bowel distal to the peritoneal reflection (from the sacral promontory to the upper edge of the symphysis pubis as determined by MRI).
Circumferential resection margin (CRM) is a critical pathologic predictor of local recurrence; an involved or threatened CRM is defined as tumor within 1 mm of the resected margin.

Total neoadjuvant therapy (TNT) is now the preferred approach for stage II-III rectal cancer, offering higher rates of pathologic complete response, improved treatment completion, and potential for organ preservation.

For dMMR/MSI-H or POLE/POLD1-mutated tumors, neoadjuvant checkpoint inhibitor immunotherapy is the preferred first-line treatment for stage II-III disease, often leading to clinical complete response and avoidance of surgery.
The addition of low-dose aspirin (100-162 mg daily for 3 years) after surgery is recommended for stage II-III tumors with somatic PI3K pathway alterations (PIK3CA mutations, PTEN loss).

Nonoperative management (watch-and-wait) may be considered for patients with a clinical complete response after neoadjuvant therapy, but only at centers with experienced multidisciplinary teams and with the understanding of the increased risk of local regrowth, which is highest in the first 2-3 years.
Surveillance after curative-intent treatment should be stage-stratified, with more intensive follow-up (CEA, imaging) for higher stages (II-IV) to detect potentially resectable recurrences.

guidelines_resources.notes: Provides evidence-based recommendations for diagnosis, staging, treatment, and surveillance of rectal cancer. Updated regularly.

guidelines_resources.notes: Includes the Distress Thermometer and Problem List to screen for psychosocial distress.

guidelines_resources.title: Colorectal Cancer Survivorship Care Guidelines

key_trials.full_name: Short-course radiotherapy followed by chemotherapy before total mesorectal excision versus preoperative chemoradiotherapy, TME, and optional adjuvant chemotherapy in locally advanced rectal cancer
key_trials.population: Patients with locally advanced rectal cancer (high-risk features)
key_trials.intervention: Short-course RT (25 Gy/5 fractions) followed by CAPEOX or FOLFOX, then TME (experimental arm)
key_trials.comparator: Standard neoadjuvant long-course chemoRT (50.4 Gy with concurrent capecitabine or 5-FU), then TME, with optional adjuvant chemotherapy
key_trials.key_result: 3-year disease-related treatment failure rate was 23.7% with TNT vs. 30.4% with standard treatment (HR 0.75; 95% CI 0.60-0.95; P = .019).
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Pathologic complete response (pCR) rate was 28% vs. 14%. No difference in overall survival. 5-year follow-up showed a higher locoregional recurrence rate with TNT (10% vs. 6%, P = .027).
key_trials.practice_change: Established total neoadjuvant therapy (TNT) with short-course RT followed by chemotherapy as a standard of care for locally advanced rectal cancer, improving disease-free survival.

key_trials.full_name: Neoadjuvant chemotherapy with FOLFIRINOX and preoperative chemoradiotherapy for patients with locally advanced rectal cancer
key_trials.population: Patients with locally advanced rectal cancer (cT3-4, N+)
key_trials.intervention: Neoadjuvant mFOLFIRINOX (6 cycles) followed by long-course chemoRT (50.4 Gy with capecitabine), then TME
key_trials.comparator: Standard neoadjuvant long-course chemoRT, then TME
key_trials.key_result: 3-year DFS was 76% in the TNT group vs. 69% in the standard group (HR 0.69; 95% CI 0.49-0.97; P = .034).
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: 7-year DFS 67.6% vs. 62.5%; 7-year OS 81.9% vs. 76.1%. Serious adverse events were lower in the TNT group (11% vs. 23%, P = .0049).
key_trials.practice_change: Supported the use of intensive induction chemotherapy (FOLFIRINOX) as part of a TNT strategy for locally advanced rectal cancer, improving DFS.

key_trials.full_name: Preoperative treatment of locally advanced rectal cancer
key_trials.population: Patients with stage II or III rectal cancer (cT2, N1-2 or cT3, N0-2) who were candidates for sphincter-sparing surgery
key_trials.intervention: Neoadjuvant chemotherapy with mFOLFOX6 (12 cycles), with selective use of chemoRT if tumor regression ≤20%
key_trials.comparator: Neoadjuvant long-course chemoRT (50.4 Gy with concurrent 5-FU or capecitabine)
key_trials.key_result: Noninferiority was established (HR 0.92; 95% CI 0.74-1.14; P = .005 for noninferiority). 5-year DFS was 80.8% with FOLFOX vs. 78.6% with chemoRT.
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Similar rates of locoregional recurrence, overall survival, and pathologic complete response. Patient-reported outcomes showed worse short-term AEs with chemoRT but worse long-term AEs (fatigue, neuropathy, sexual function) with FOLFOX.
key_trials.practice_change: Demonstrated that selective omission of chemoRT after a favorable response to neoadjuvant chemotherapy is a viable option for patients meeting trial criteria, allowing avoidance of radiation-related toxicity.

key_trials.full_name: Encorafenib, binimetinib, and cetuximab in BRAF V600E-mutated colorectal cancer
key_trials.population: Patients with previously treated BRAF V600E-mutant metastatic CRC
key_trials.intervention: Encorafenib + binimetinib + cetuximab (triplet) or encorafenib + cetuximab (doublet)
key_trials.comparator: Cetuximab + investigator's choice of irinotecan or FOLFIRI
key_trials.key_result: Median OS was 9.0 months with triplet, 8.4 months with doublet, and 5.4 months with control (triplet vs. control HR 0.52; 95% CI 0.39-0.70; P < .001).
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Median progression-free survival (PFS) was 4.3 months (triplet), 3.2 months (doublet), and 1.5 months (control).
key_trials.practice_change: Established encorafenib + cetuximab (with or without binimetinib) as a standard of care for previously treated BRAF V600E-mutant metastatic CRC.

key_trials.full_name: Pembrolizumab versus chemotherapy in microsatellite instability-high or mismatch repair-deficient metastatic colorectal cancer
key_trials.population: Patients with previously untreated MSI-H/dMMR metastatic CRC
key_trials.comparator: Investigator's choice of chemotherapy (FOLFOX or FOLFIRI) with or without bevacizumab or cetuximab
key_trials.key_result: Median PFS was 8.2 months with pembrolizumab vs. 5.9 months with chemotherapy (HR 0.60; 95% CI 0.45-0.80; P = .001).
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Objective response rate (ORR) 43.8% vs. 33.1%. 5-year OS rate was 53.9% vs. 35.5%.
key_trials.practice_change: Established pembrolizumab as a first-line standard of care for MSI-H/dMMR metastatic CRC, improving PFS and offering durable responses.

key_trials.full_name: Nivolumab in patients with metastatic DNA mismatch repair-deficient/microsatellite instability-high colorectal cancer
key_trials.population: Patients with dMMR/MSI-H metastatic CRC (mostly previously treated)
key_trials.intervention: Nivolumab 3 mg/kg every 2 weeks ± ipilimumab 1 mg/kg every 3 weeks for 4 doses
key_trials.key_result: ORR was 31% for nivolumab monotherapy and 55% for the combination. 12-month OS rate was 73%.
key_trials.practice_change: Provided early evidence for the efficacy of nivolumab (± ipilimumab) in dMMR/MSI-H metastatic CRC, leading to FDA approval.
